title: Count Failed SSH Login Users
hook: Failed SSH attempts are noisy; grouping users makes the pattern readable.
problem: You need to count which usernames are being targeted in SSH failures.
command: sed -n 's/.*Failed password for \(invalid user \)\?\([^ ]*\) from .*/\2/p' logs/auth.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr
starting_state: An auth log contains failed SSH password attempts against several usernames.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command extracts usernames and counts repeats.
when_to_use: Use during SSH brute-force triage or when checking which accounts are being probed.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat it as a complete incident timeline; it only summarizes matching log lines.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A count-sorted list of usernames from failed SSH attempts.

commands: sed -n 's/.*Failed password for \(invalid user \)\?\([^ ]*\) from .*/\2/p' logs/auth.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr

shorts.title: Count failed SSH users.
shorts.voiceover: Instead of reading every auth line, extract the failed usernames and count which accounts are being targeted.
shorts.caption: Group SSH failures by username.

linkedin.question: Do you group failed SSH attempts by username during first response?
linkedin.cta: Summarize the auth log before reading every line.

youtube.description: Count targeted usernames from failed SSH password lines.

title: Count Failed SSH Login IPs
hook: The loudest SSH source is usually visible with one count.
problem: You need to rank source IPs from failed SSH login attempts.
command: sed -n 's/.*Failed password .* from \([0-9.]*\) port.*/\1/p' logs/auth.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr
starting_state: An auth log contains failed SSH attempts from multiple documentation-range IPs.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command extracts source IPs and counts repeats.
when_to_use: Use when deciding whether one source is causing most SSH noise.
when_not_to_use: Do not block IPs from this output alone without considering NATs, allowlists, and policy.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A count-sorted list of source IP addresses from failed SSH attempts.

commands: sed -n 's/.*Failed password .* from \([0-9.]*\) port.*/\1/p' logs/auth.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr

shorts.title: Rank failed SSH IPs.
shorts.voiceover: Extract source IPs from failed SSH lines and count them to find the loudest source.
shorts.caption: Find the loudest SSH source.

linkedin.question: Do you rank SSH failure sources before touching firewall rules?
linkedin.cta: Use counts as evidence, then apply policy.

youtube.description: Rank source IPs from failed SSH password attempts.

title: Show Accepted SSH Logins
hook: During first response, successful logins matter more than background noise.
problem: You need to find successful SSH public-key logins in an auth log.
starting_state: An auth log contains both failed and accepted SSH events.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command filters successful public-key login lines.
when_to_use: Use when building a quick access timeline or checking unexpected SSH access.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume password logins are absent unless you also search other Accepted patterns.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Accepted public-key login lines including user and source IP.

shorts.title: Find successful SSH logins.
shorts.voiceover: In first response, filter accepted SSH logins so real access stands apart from failed noise.
shorts.caption: Successful logins are timeline anchors.

linkedin.question: Do you separate successful SSH logins from failed noise first?
linkedin.cta: Build access timelines from accepted login lines.

youtube.description: Filter accepted SSH public-key logins from auth logs.

title: Show Recent sudo Commands
hook: Privilege use is one of the fastest first-response signals.
problem: You need to see recent sudo commands from auth logs.
starting_state: An auth log contains sudo command audit lines.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command filters sudo lines and shows the newest matches in file order.
when_to_use: Use during incident triage or after unexpected service changes.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume all privileged actions use sudo; also inspect service logs and shell history where appropriate.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Recent sudo log lines including user, working directory, target user, and command.

shorts.title: Review recent sudo use.
shorts.voiceover: Filter sudo lines from auth logs to see recent privileged commands and who ran them.
shorts.caption: Privilege use is a first-response clue.

linkedin.question: Do you check sudo logs when a VPS changes unexpectedly?
linkedin.cta: Privilege-use logs are fast timeline anchors.

youtube.description: Find recent sudo commands in auth logs.

title: List Listening Ports on a VPS
hook: Unexpected network listeners are first-response evidence.
problem: You need a snapshot of TCP ports listening on the server.
starting_state: A VPS has SSH, web, and local database sockets.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command lists listening TCP sockets and processes.
when_to_use: Use during exposure checks, incident triage, or after deploying services.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat a listener as externally reachable without checking firewall and bind address.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Listening sockets with local addresses, ports, and process names where available.

shorts.title: List VPS listening ports.
shorts.voiceover: Use ss to snapshot listening ports, bind addresses, and processes before changing firewall rules.
shorts.caption: Listeners first, firewall second.

linkedin.question: Do you check bind addresses before assuming a port is exposed?
linkedin.cta: Pair ss output with firewall checks.

youtube.description: Use ss -ltnp to inspect listening TCP ports on a VPS.

title: List Users with Login Shells
hook: Not every local account should be able to log in.
problem: You need to list accounts with shell-like login programs.
starting_state: A passwd-style file contains service accounts and human accounts.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reads account records and prints shell users.
when_to_use: Use during account inventory, server handoff, or first-response checks.
when_not_to_use: Do not disable accounts from this output alone; confirm ownership and operational use first.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Usernames and login shells for accounts ending in sh.

linkedin.question: Do you inventory login-capable users during VPS handoff?
linkedin.cta: Review shell accounts before changing access.

title: Check Key SSH Authentication Settings
hook: SSH policy should be visible before you change it.
problem: You need to read important SSH daemon authentication settings.
starting_state: An sshd_config file contains root login, password, pubkey, and allow-list settings.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints matching SSH configuration lines with numbers.
when_to_use: Use during SSH hardening checks or after unexpected login behavior.
when_not_to_use: Do not reload SSH from this output alone; validate full config syntax in real systems.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.

title: Find World-Writable Web Directories
hook: World-writable web paths deserve immediate review.
problem: You need to find directories under a web root that anyone can write to.
starting_state: A web root contains upload and private directories with different modes.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints directories with the world-writable bit set.
when_to_use: Use during server hardening, web incident triage, or after upload-path changes.
when_not_to_use: Do not chmod paths blindly; confirm application requirements and ownership first.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.

shorts.title: Find writable web dirs.

title: Find Loose Private Key Permissions
hook: SSH private keys should not be readable like ordinary files.
problem: You need to find private-key-looking files with modes broader than 600.
starting_state: Home directories contain SSH files with mixed permissions.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints key-looking files whose numeric mode is greater than 600.
when_to_use: Use during server access audits or after provisioning SSH credentials.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume every id_* file is a real private key; inspect carefully before changing or deleting.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Mode and path for private-key-looking files with loose permissions.

shorts.title: Find loose SSH keys.
shorts.voiceover: List id underscore files and flag modes broader than 600 so risky private key permissions stand out.
shorts.caption: Private keys should be tightly permissioned.

linkedin.question: Do you audit SSH key file modes during VPS access reviews?
linkedin.cta: Find risky key permissions before changing access.

youtube.description: Find private-key-looking SSH files with loose permissions.

title: List authorized_keys Files
hook: Authorized keys are the server's practical access list.
problem: You need to find authorized_keys files and their modes.
starting_state: Home directories contain SSH authorized_keys files.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints matching files with permissions.
when_to_use: Use during access inventory, server handoff, or after removing a user.
when_not_to_use: Do not remove keys from this output alone; confirm ownership and active dependencies first.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: File modes and paths for authorized_keys files.

shorts.title: Inventory authorized_keys.
shorts.voiceover: Find authorized keys files and modes so you know where SSH access is configured.
shorts.caption: authorized_keys is practical access inventory.

linkedin.question: Do you inventory authorized_keys files during server handoff?
linkedin.cta: Review SSH access files before removing users.
